highlights.title: API Separation & Prefetching
highlights.problem: The user info API called on the main screen took 2–3 seconds, delaying the entire page load by the same amount.
highlights.solution:
Designed a lightweight API with the backend team, selecting only the data needed for the main screen.
Shifted the heavy existing API to root-level rendering so it runs as background prefetching.
Refactored other pages that were also redundantly calling the same API.
highlights.result: Main screen load time: 2–3s → 0.6s. Pages with redundant calls refactored to near-zero or 0.3–0.4s.

highlights.title: E2E Testing & Reinforcement Learning Integration
highlights.problem: Some features required visual inspection, making automated testing impossible with existing methods.
highlights.solution:
Mock support made E2E testing feasible, so introduced E2E (Detox) and Jest.
Wrote test code focused on key features and components.
For tests requiring visual judgment, integrated a CNN model trained with reinforcement learning.
highlights.result: Automated tests for core features and components; reduced error occurrence rate.

highlights.title: Duplicate API Removal & Cache Management with Tanstack Query
highlights.problem: APIs fetching rarely-changing values on every focus event and APIs dependent on special states were causing dozens of redundant requests per session.
highlights.solution:
Identified APIs re-fetching on every focus and those tied only to special states.
Configured Tanstack Query's stale time to block unnecessary re-fetches.
After mutations, used query client to mark related data as stale so it refetches only when needed.
Removed complex useEffect-based state management code.
highlights.result: API calls per session: dozens → 3–4; improved state management productivity.

highlights.title: API Mock Testing Feature
highlights.problem: No way to arbitrarily change frontend UI state; test account limitations made diverse scenario testing impossible.
highlights.solution:
Introduced API Mock Adaptor and implemented internal state control features for key app states.
Added mock coverage for APIs accounting for 80% of usage by log volume.
highlights.result: Increased dev productivity and reduced bug occurrence rate compared to previous workflow.

highlights.title: Bundle Optimization
highlights.problem: Test code added to the project was being included in production builds, inflating bundle size.
highlights.solution:
Used babel.config and metro.config to automatically replace test-related source files with stub files at build time.
Removed console-related code and other production-unnecessary code.
Verified optimization results using atlas and hbc map to analyze bundle output.

highlights.title: Legacy Code Cleanup & Expo Migration
highlights.problem: Play Store 16kb page issue, legacy library conflicts, and duplicated functions/components were increasing development time.
highlights.solution:
Migrated Expo 52 → 54 to resolve the 16kb issue, removing unsupported libraries and fixing dependent code.
Extracted repeated logic from key files into shared functions, hooks, and components via refactoring.
highlights.result: Page-level source code reduced to 1/4; improved maintainability and readability.

highlights.title: Deployment Pipeline Setup
highlights.problem: Need arose to add automated testing to the deployment process and enable real-time OTA deployment, but no pipeline existed.
highlights.solution:
Added automated test scripts via npm commands and configured EAS build and deploy.
Added EAS channels for OTA updates and distributed an internal test app.
Configured internal test builds via babel.config and metro.config to include mock features in the bundle.
Built a 4-stage pipeline: local → internal test → final test → release.
highlights.result: Incident/bug response time: 2 days (iOS App Store review cycle) → 1 hour.

aiHighlights.title: Designing and Operating a Multi-Agent Harness for Feature Implementation and Bug Analysis
aiHighlights.summary: Designed and operated two harnesses, Testing Harness and Feature Harness, using an orchestrator-designer skill built on Claude Code. Compared with a single-agent setup, it improved detection of boundary-condition defects and established a spec → implementation → verification pipeline.
aiHighlights.context:
When implementing features with a single agent, defects such as race conditions in screen transitions and state management occurred with some frequency.
In a multi-agent setup, cases of unexpected defects being identified were repeatedly observed.
There was a need to lock planning, implementation, and bug analysis into a fixed pipeline.
aiHighlights.approach:
Using a self-built orchestrator-designer skill on Claude Code, two harnesses were designed and operated.
The Testing Harness, which does not affect live features and is dedicated to bug analysis and cross-checking, was built first.
Then the Feature Harness was constructed for spec → design → simulation → conclusion → implementation → verification.
The Testing Harness was integrated into the verification stage of the Feature Harness, forming a single pipeline.
aiHighlights.verification:
By reviewing errors found during testing, the harness re-examined related areas and surfaced derivative defects.
Some of the harness's diagnoses were discarded or revised by a human.
As a result, a complex app-bootstrap component was decomposed into multiple hooks with clearer responsibilities, and related tests were added.
aiHighlights.impact:
Before and after adoption, personal metrics were measured at 5.17 → 9.25 commits/day (+79%) and 0.71 → 2.06 merged PRs/day (+190%).
The fix/feat ratio changed from 1.53 → 2.05, with no meaningful decrease confirmed.
Qualitatively, boundary-condition defects were caught more often, and reusable assets accumulated through documentation of the spec → implementation → verification flow.

aiHighlights.title: Building Jest Tests and Business Rule Documentation in Parallel
aiHighlights.summary: Built Jest unit and component tests alongside per-feature business rule documents with Claude Code. Established a system with 217 tests and 11 documents, where test failures function as a way to trace rule changes.
aiHighlights.context: The app lacked unit and component tests, so deployment stability needed to be improved. Adding tests was chosen as a way to secure quality without heavily modifying feature code. As the number of tests grew, it became harder to trace the relationship between rules and tests. The setup was expanded into a structure that manages business rule documents and a test catalog together.
aiHighlights.approach:
Claude Code was used as the primary tool.
Business rule extraction was handled by a human, test case candidate generation was done jointly by human and AI, and AI wrote the test code and per-feature documents.
Existing E2E tests and predefined types were provided as reference material to keep conventions consistent.
Instead of generating everything at once, tests were added sequentially by feature to keep each change reviewable.
aiHighlights.verification:
AI-written feature documents were reviewed first to confirm alignment with the rule definitions.
The implementation stage was handled by the Feature Harness, so the initial failure rate was low.
Afterward, the main type of failure came from existing tests breaking during code-structure changes.
As of now, there has been no confirmed case of tests proactively catching a production bug. A systematic way to validate test quality itself remains an open task.
aiHighlights.impact:
Starting from no test cases at all, the suite grew to 20 suites / 217 tests.
It consists of 11 feature documents and 21 test files.
Writing 5 new E2E tests used to take 1-2 weeks, but after introducing AI, roughly 200 tests were completed in 2 weeks.
By fixing business rules as document-test pairs, it became possible to secure stability through verification during new feature development and refactoring.
